citation.title: Microstructure-engineered carbon-silica-zirconia membrane for highly efficient methanol pervaporation recovery

content: A B S T R A C T Carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 (C–SiO2–ZrO2) membranes are promising candidates for MeOH/solvent separations via per­vaporation (PV) owing to their outstanding molecular sieving properties, tunable surface characteristics, and chemical stability. However, their dense structure and small pore sizes require deliberate microstructural design for effective MeOH recovery. In this study, benzoylformic acid (BFA) was used as an organic chelating ligand to engineer the C–SiO2–ZrO2 membrane pore structure, with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and zirconium (IV) tert-butoxide (ZrTB) as precursors. The rigid phenyl groups in BFA facilitated the formation of a loosely structured SiO2–ZrO2 network via steric hindrance and π-π interaction. Carbonization of the APTES-ZrTB-BFA (AZB) material released the pore structure occupied by organic moieties and enhanced the hydrophilicity of the membrane. The AZB membrane carbonized at 700 ◦C combined a uniform, loose microporous structure with a hydrophilic surface, achieving MeOH permeance surpassing 10-7 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and selectivities of 120 and 1200 for MeOH/DMC and MeOH/toluene, respectively, in the PV process. This work provides valuable insights into tailoring the microstructure of C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes for challenging sub-nanometer separations.

citation.title: Microstructure-engineered carbon-silica-zirconia membrane for highly efficient methanol pervaporation recovery

content: 1. Introduction Methanol (MeOH) is a commonly used solvent in industries such as petroleum, pharmaceuticals, and petrochemicals, and is recognized as one of the most environmentally friendly fuels [1–3]. However, a considerable amount of MeOH is released into the environment as waste, which has detrimental effects on the economy, environment, and public health. For instance, in the United States alone, approximately 14 million tons of MeOH were utilized for waste management in 2019, with approximately 818,258 tons discharged without adequate purification, according to data reported by the United States Environmental Protec­tion Agency (EPA) [4]. In industrial processes for organic solvent pro­duction, MeOH forms azeotropic mixtures with other compounds, such as MeOH/dimethyl carbonate (MeOH/DMC, an azeotropic mixture with a 70/30 wt % ratio) and MeOH/toluene (an azeotropic mixture with a 72/28 wt % ratio), which complicates the separation process [5]. Furthermore, the costs associated with the recycling and disposal of solvents are comparable to the expenses of purchasing new solvents, highlighting the critical need for MeOH recovery, recycling, and reuse for economic and environmental sustainability [6].

citation.title: Microstructure-engineered carbon-silica-zirconia membrane for highly efficient methanol pervaporation recovery

content: of purchasing new solvents, highlighting the critical need for MeOH recovery, recycling, and reuse for economic and environmental sustainability [6]. Conventional separation techniques, including extractive, azeo­tropic, and pressure-swing distillation, are energy-intensive and economically inefficient due to their substantial capital and operational costs, which account for approximately 10–15 % of the global energy consumption [7]. Therefore, the development of separation technolo­gies that combine energy and process optimization for efficient recovery and recycling of valuable products from waste streams is essential for achieving economic viability and environmental sustainability. Mem­brane separation has emerged as a promising alternative to conventional energy-intensive techniques because of its superior operational simplicity, energy efficiency, and environmental benefits. Pervaporation (PV) technology, a membrane-based method that offers both energy efficiency and eco-friendliness, enables the separation of the majority of the feed without pre-vaporization, thereby reducing the latent heat re­quirements and significantly enhancing energy efficiency [8]. There­fore, PV displays considerable advantages over traditional separation methods, particularly in lower energy consumption, reduced carbon footprint, and superior capability in processing organic solvent

The development of advanced membrane materials to achieve su­perior separation performance is critical for facilitating the industrial application of PV processes. Hydrophilic organic polymers such as poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA), chitosan (CS), polyamide (PA), and cellulose ac­etate (CA) have been employed for the PV separation of MeOH/organic solvent mixtures due to their high hydrophilicity and ease of membrane fabrication [9,11–13]. However, despite these early advancements, polymer membranes continue to face challenges such as low mechanical strength and pronounced swelling during long-term operation, which compromise their separation efficiency. Furthermore, conventional polymer-based membranes suffer from the permeability-selectivity trade-off [14]. Inorganic sub-nanoporous membranes, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), zeolites, and amorphous silica (SiO2), exhibit superior chemical stability and enhanced separation performance, demonstrating advantages in the PV separation of MeOH [15–19]. However, MOFs and zeolite membranes typically exhibit high brittleness, making them susceptible to cracking.

citation.title: Microstructure-engineered carbon-silica-zirconia membrane for highly efficient methanol pervaporation recovery

content: ges in the PV separation of MeOH [15–19]. However, MOFs and zeolite membranes typically exhibit high brittleness, making them susceptible to cracking. Consequently, the development of high-quality PV membranes that combine exceptional separation performance with pronounced stability for MeOH recovery remains a critical area of ongoing research. Silica-based membranes, with their inherent molecular sieving properties and relatively flexible amorphous structures, offer a viable solution [20,21]. Despite the tendency of conventional silica membranes to densify under high-temperature and humid conditions owing to their structural disorder and the presence of terminal hydroxyl groups [22], ceramic composite membranes that integrate SiO2 with stable metal oxides, especially zirconium oxide (ZrO2), through the sol-gel process demonstrate excellent hydrothermal and chemical stability [23]. On this basis, the modification of the ZrO2 precursor with organic chelating li­gands (OCLs) allows the consumption of alkoxy groups on its side chain, thereby regulating its hydrolysis rate and achieving a homogeneous SiO2–ZrO2 composition [24]. This approach significantly enhances the stability of the membrane while preserving its inherent separation per­formance.

citation.title: Microstructure-engineered carbon-silica-zirconia membrane for highly efficient methanol pervaporation recovery

content: SiO2–ZrO2 composition [24]. This approach significantly enhances the stability of the membrane while preserving its inherent separation per­formance. Furthermore, through pyrolysis in an inert atmosphere, the organic components in the OCL-modified SiO2–ZrO2 (OCL-SiO2-ZrO2) are converted into carbon structures, resulting in carbon-SiO2-ZrO2 (C–SiO2–ZrO2) composite membranes that exhibit high porosity and superior hydrophilicity. Previous research demonstrates that OCL-­SiO2-ZrO2 with crosslinked organic moieties, characterized by their reduced mobility, enhances the uniformity of carbon distribution following pyrolysis and mitigates defect formation during membrane fabrication, which leads to an outstanding hydrothermal stability and molecular sieving performance [25,26]. These results suggest that C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes are promising candidates for MeOH separation. However, to date, the organic pre­cursors used for C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes have been based on linear alkyl structures. Although linear organic moieties with the crosslinked structure help control carbon distribution and reduce defect formation in C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes, the dense packing of the SiO2–ZrO2 network will not be constrained due to the inherently flexible nature of the linear organic compounds. The reported C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes, therefore, exhibited dense structures with small pore sizes, limiting their applications primarily to H2 separation and PV dehydration [21,26].

content: ranes, therefore, exhibited dense structures with small pore sizes, limiting their applications primarily to H2 separation and PV dehydration [21,26]. To address this, we propose that designing OCLs with nonlinear and rigid side chain structures at the molecular level is essential to promote the loose assembly of the SiO2–ZrO2 network and obtain C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes with large and uniform micropores suitable for precise MeOH separation. Herein, benzoylformic acid (BFA) with a rigid phenyl group on side chains was deliberately selected as the OCL, while 3-aminopropyltrie­thoxysilane (APTES) and zirconium (IV) tert-butoxide (ZrTB) were selected as the precursors for Si and Zr, respectively, to design a nonlinear organic moiety with a rigid side group for the fabrication of C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes toward the PV separation of MeOH/organic solvents. As shown in Fig. 1a, a crosslinked organic backbone can be obtained from the aldimine condensation in the obtained APTES-ZrTBBFA (AZB) sol, which will optimize the distribution of free carbon during pyrolysis and promote a uniform pore size distribution, ensuring the molecular sieving ability [25]. Additionally, the randomly distrib­uted phenyl groups on BFA act as rigid molecular spacers, inducing the loose arrangement of the SiO2–ZrO2 network during the sol-gel process

Carbonization of the organic components releases the network pores, resulting in a loose membrane structure for MeOH/organic solvent separation. Notably, the AZB membrane carbonized at 700 ◦C (AZB-700) exhibited exceptional MeOH separation performance, benefiting from the synergistic effects of adsorption-diffusion and molecular sieving mechanisms, aided by the hydrophilic surface and loose pore structure. Our results demonstrate that nonlinear organic moieties derived from OCLs with rigid side chains facilitate the formation of the loose C–SiO2–ZrO2 network, offering new insights for designing C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes with precisely tailored pore structures for broader separa­tion applications. 2. Methanol (MeOH, 99.5 %), ethanol (EtOH, 99.5 %), isopropanol (IPA, 99.5 %), ethyl acetate (EA, 99.5 %), dimethyl carbonate (DMC, 99.5 %), toluene (99.5 %), and glyoxylic acid monohydrate (GA, 95 %) were obtained from FUJIFILM Pure Chemical Corporation (Japan). Acetic acid (99.0 %) and nitric acid (HNO3, 60 %) were obtained from Nacalai Tesque, Inc. (Japan). Porous α-alumina (Al2O3) tubular substrates with 60 % porosity, a length of 10 cm, an outer diameter of 1 cm, and an average pore size of 1.2 μm were supplied by Nikkato Co. (Japan). 2.2. Sol preparation In this study, two types of sols were synthesized, one containing the phenyl group (APTES-ZrTB-BFA, denoted as AZB) and the other without (APTES-ZrTB-GA, denoted as AZG), as illustrated in Fig. 1. Both sols were prepared via identical procedures, differing only in OCLs employed. Briefly, ZrTB was dissolved in EtOH and subsequently modified with either BFA or GA at an OCL/ZrTB molar ratio of 4:1. Subsequently, the ZrTB-OCL solution was added dropwise to the predissolved APTES in EtOH for an APTES/ZrTB molar ratio of 4:1. After thorough mixing, deionized water and acetic acid were introduced into the system, maintaining an alkoxide/H2O/acetic acid molar ratio of 1:4:0.3.

The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature at 550 rpm for 12 h to facilitate the hydrolysis, co-condensation, and aldimine condensation reactions, and the sol concentration was maintained at 5 wt %. 2.3. Membrane, film, and xerogel powders preparation The fabricated membrane consists of a porous α-Al2O3 substrate, particle layer, intermediate layer, and separation layer. To fabricate the particle layer, a nonwoven fabric was wetted with a 10 wt % dispersion of 1–2 μm α-Al2O3 particles in a Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol, which was prepared following established procedures (as described in Text S1 of the Sup­porting Information) [27]. This dispersion was applied onto the α-Al2O3 substrate, followed by air-drying and calcination at 800 ◦C for 20 min. Then, a dispersion of 0.2 μm α-Al2O3 particles in Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol was applied using the same procedure. Both coating steps were repeated several times to smooth the surface and cover pinholes or defects on the substrate. The intermediate layer was prepared by coating a diluted Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol (0.5 wt %). The substrate was heated to 200 ◦C, fol­lowed by coating of the Y–SiO2–ZrO2 sol wetted nonwoven fabric, and the membrane was calcined at 800 ◦C for 20 min.

The membrane was heated to 200 ◦C, coated with the sol-wetted nonwoven fabric, and then carbonized at 550, 700, or 800 ◦C under N2 for 30 min. The obtained membranes were denoted as AZB-x and AZG-x, where x represents the calcination temperature. The AZB-/AZG-derived films were prepared on Si wafers, which were pretreated with ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, by spin-coating, followed by calcination at specified temperatures. To obtain the xerogel powders, the AZB/AZG sol was subjected to slow solvent evaporation at 50–80 ◦C in air, followed by carbonization under N2 at predetermined tempera­tures. Detailed characterization methods are provided in Text S2 of the Supporting Information. 2.4. Gas and PV performance measurement The physical properties of gas and solvent molecules used for mea­surements are listed in Table S1. The single-gas permeation performance of AZG membranes was evaluated at 200 ◦C for He, H2, CO2, N2, CH4, CF4, and SF6, with the setup illustrated in Fig. S1a. A pressure differ­ential of ~200 kPa was maintained across the membrane using a backpressure valve, while the permeate flow rate was measured using a soap film flowmeter. Before measurements, the membrane was pre­conditioned at 200 ◦C under a 20 mL min-1 N2 flow for 12 h to eliminate any residual moisture. Each test was conducted at least five times to ensure reliability.

Effects of OCLs on the microstructure and thermal stability of C–SiO2–ZrO2 materials To evaluate the effect of different OCLs on the C–SiO2–ZrO2 struc­ture, a series of chemical structure characterizations were conducted on AZB and AZG. Previous studies have confirmed the role of GA in modifying ZrTB [25]. In this study, we explored the modification effect of BFA on ZrTB through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), as shown in Fig. 2a. The FTIR spectrum of BFA displayed characteristic peaks corresponding to the C––O groups in the carboxyl and aldehyde groups at 1755 and 1665 cm-1, the C–O bond of the aldehyde group at 1240 cm-1, and the aromatic ring vibrations at 1600 and 1430 cm-1 [28–31]. Compared with the pristine ZrTB, the BFA-modified ZrTB (ZrTB-BFA) exhibited characteristic aromatic ring and C–O bond peaks from BFA. Additionally, the C––O peak of the carboxyl group at 1755 cm-1 was almost absent in ZrTB-BFA, and a new peak appeared in the range of 1600–1650 cm-1, which was ascribed to the carboxylate-metal complexes [32], indicating the successful modification of ZrTB by the carboxyl group on BFA. Fig. 2b presents the FTIR spectra of the uncal­cined AZG and AZB films. For the AZB sample, the characteristic peaks associated with C––O bonds and carboxylate-metal complexes were replaced by a peak in the 1500–1720 cm-1 range, indicative of imine bond formation [33].

content: sociated with C––O bonds and carboxylate-metal complexes were replaced by a peak in the 1500–1720 cm-1 range, indicative of imine bond formation [33]. Additionally, a distinct peak around 1070 cm-1, corresponding to Si–O–Si bonds in a SiO2-like network structure [34,35], was observed. The AZB and AZG exhibited nearly identical FTIR spectra, with the exception of a characteristic peak at 1430 cm-1 attributed to aromatic ring vibrations was observed in AZB. Notably, the peak at 1755 cm-1, which was also related to aromatic ring vibrations, was unobservable in the AZB sample due to being obscured by the imine bond peak. To further elucidate the chemical structures of the obtained mate­rials, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. 2c, almost no difference was observed in the N 1s spectra for both AZG and AZB samples, since the N element originated from the amine groups on APTES and underwent the same reaction process. The deconvoluted peaks in both samples at 398.9, 400.3, and 402.1 eV correspond to the imine (C––N) bond, C–N bond, and incom­pletely reacted –NH2 groups, respectively. These results provide direct evidence for the progression of aldimine condensation and the forma­tion of crosslinked organic moieties in both the AZG and AZB samples. Fig. 2d shows the C 1s XPS profiles of the AZB and AZG samples.

Before measurement, both samples were kept at 100 ◦C under N2 flow for 2 h to eliminate any adsorbed moisture. However, the weight loss observed for AZB was greater than that of AZG, which could be attributed to the phenyl groups present in the organic chains of AZB. All samples exhibited a broad amorphous peak in the 15–35◦range, corresponding to the Si–O–Zr lattice. Upon further inspection, AZB-250 exhibited a significantly narrower full width at half-maximum (FWHM) and larger d-spacing than AZG-250, indicating a more ordered and loosely packed lattice structure [36]. This can be attributed to the repulsive and π-π interaction between the randomly distributed phenyl groups, which will serve as the rigid molecular spacer for the network structure, in the nonlinear BFA side chains. The d-spacing decreased and the FWHM increased with increasing calcina­tion temperature for all AZG and AZB samples, suggesting a densified ceramic network structure and a more disordered structure due to the transformation of the rigid crosslinked organic moieties to amorphous carbon [37]. However, the d-spacings of the AZB samples remained larger than those of the AZG samples at all temperatures, indicating looser structures.

No tetragonal ZrO2 peaks were observed in the XRD spectra of the AZB samples [38], and the amorphous structure remained even after calcination at 700 ◦C, confirming the homogeneous SiO2–ZrO2 network structure. After assessing the pyrolysis behavior and phase information of the AZG and AZB materials, N2 adsorption was applied to reveal the dif­ferences in the microstructures of C–SiO2–ZrO2 derived from different OCLs, as shown in Fig. 3c. The AZG sample carbonized at 550 ◦C exhibited typical type I isotherms, indicating a microporous structure [39,40]. However, as the carbonization temperature increased to 700 ◦C, N2 adsorption became negligible for AZG-700, and the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore volume decreased significantly, suggesting a densification into a non-porous structure (Table 1). In contrast, AZB samples carbonized at both 550 ◦C and 700 ◦C exhibited type I isotherms, indicating the microporous domi­nance networks. These results demon­strate that a loose C–SiO2–ZrO2 network derived from the AZB material can be obtained. Given the similar molecular sizes of N2 (0.364 nm) and MeOH (0.38 nm), AZB-derived membranes show good potential for MeOH separation. Additionally, pore size analysis based on the normalized Knudsen permeance (NKP) in relation to the kinetic diameter (details can be found in Text S3 in the Supporting Information) revealed that AZB-550 (0.75 nm) had a larger pore size than AZG-550 (0.55 nm) (Fig. S3). These results collaboratively demonstrate that phenylcontaining OCL effectively act as rigid network spacer and carbon pre­cursor, resulting in a microporous C–SiO2–ZrO2 membrane with large pore structure for sub-nanometer-scale molecular separation. 3.2. The impacts of calcination temperature on the physicochemical properties of the AZB material Having demonstrated the influence of various OCLs on the C–SiO2–ZrO2 network structure and the large pore size of AZB-derived membranes, we further investigated the impact of carbonization tem­perature on the structural and surface properties of the AZB material. Given that the AZB material retained its porous structure upon calci­nation at 700 ◦C, carbonization temperatures of 550, 700, and 800 ◦C were selected for further analysis. Fig. 4a presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and electron diffraction patterns of AZB xerogel powders carbonized at different temperatures.

All samples dis­played uniform structures without phase boundaries, accompanied by broad electron diffraction halos. These findings suggest that the homo­geneous composition of SiO2–ZrO2 and the free carbon generated during pyrolysis is uniformly integrated into the ceramic matrix, effectively preserving the amorphous structure even at 800 ◦C. Fig. 4b shows the Raman spectra of AZB xerogel powders carbonized at different temperatures. The spectra for all carbonized AZB samples exhibited a D peak at 1340 cm-1, corresponding to disordered carbon, and a G peak at 1580 cm-1, corresponding to ordered graphitic carbon [42]. Notably, the intensity ratio of the D and G peaks (ID/IG) decreased with increasing carbonization temperature, suggesting a more ordered ultramicroporous graphitic carbon structure. Fig. 4c presents the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of AZB xerogel powders carbonized at 550, 700, and 800 ◦C. The N2 adsorption capacity and BET surface areas decreased with higher carbonization temperature, with the AZB-800 exhibiting a non-porous character. Although AZB-700 exhibited slightly lower N2 adsorption capacity and specific surface area compared to AZB-550, its pore size distribution was notably more uniform (Fig. S4).

Carbon­ization at 700 ◦C fully pyrolyzes the organic components, and more ordered graphitic carbon with ultramicroporous structures formed, along with slight densification of the SiO2–ZrO2 network. This results in a more uniform microporous network, albeit with a modest reduction in microporosity. Owing to the inherent hydrophilicity of carbonized AZB and the small molecular size of MeOH, all samples demonstrated a higher adsorption capacity for MeOH than toluene. Notably, although AZB-700 displayed a slightly lower microporosity than AZB-550, it exhibited a MeOH adsorption capacity comparable to that of the latter. This can be attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity of AZB-700, which is also confirmed by the higher MeOH adsorption heat (Qst) for AZB-700 than that of AZB-550 (Fig. S6).

Although the low WCA and high Qst for MeOH (Fig. S6) confirmed the strong hy­drophilicity of AZB-800, the adsorption capacities of both MeOH and toluene in AZB-800 were lower than those of AZB-550 and AZB-700 due to the dense network structure. As depicted in Fig. 5d, AZB-550, AZB-700, and AZB-800 effectively excluded toluene and dynamically adsorbed MeOH during the initial stage of the breakthrough measurements. However, in com­parison to AZB-550 (with retention times of 89.7 min for MeOH and 11.2 min for toluene), AZB-700 exhibited a significantly longer retention time for MeOH (140.3 min) and shorter retention time for toluene (4.14 min). In contrast, AZB-800 exhibited the shortest retention times for both MeOH (28.2 min) and toluene (3.11 min). AZB-700 clearly dis­played the most pronounced MeOH/toluene adsorption separation ef­ficiency, which can be attributed to its optimal balance of high hydrophilicity, moderate microporosity, and uniform microporous structure.

All membranes featured a continuous C–SiO2–ZrO2 selective layer (less than 100 nm in thickness), which was tightly bonded to the intermediate layer. PV separation performance of MeOH/solvents for carbonized AZB membranes Fig. 7 and S9 show the PV separation performance of AZB-550, AZB700, and AZB-800 membranes for MeOH/IPA, MeOH/EA, MeOH/DMC, and MeOH/toluene (10/90 wt %) mixtures. With the exception of DMC, the MeOH/solvent selectivities and separation factors of all membranes gradually improved as the solvent molecule size increased. Both AZB550 and AZB-700 membranes exhibited lower MeOH permeance in the MeOH/IPA system compared to other MeOH/solvent mixtures. This can be attributed to the higher polarity and smaller molecular size of IPA than other solvent molecules, which facilitates its adsorption/diffusion within the membrane pores, thereby hindering MeOH permeation [47]. However, this effect was marginal for the AZB-800 membrane, which showed comparable MeOH permeance across various MeOH/solvent mixtures, attributable to the severely densified network structures. Furthermore, despite the slightly larger molecular size of DMC (0.53 nm) relative to that of EA (0.52 nm), the MeOH permeance and selec­tivity in the MeOH/DMC system were slightly lower than those observed in the MeOH/EA system for AZB-550 and AZB-700 membranes. This is likely due to the higher polarity of DMC, which tends to interact with the hydrophilic membrane surface [5,48].

These factors effectively mitigate the impact of feed concentration variations on membrane performance, as shown in Fig. 9d. Similarly, for the MeOH/toluene separation (MeOH content of 10–90 wt %), the AZB-700 membrane exhibited high MeOH permeances (3.7–4.2 * 10-7 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1) and selectivities of 1200–1800, surpassing those of various membranes reported in the literature and being comparable to that of polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS)-containing carbon-based (carbon-POSS) membranes. Notably, surface modification strategies have been shown to enhance the per­formance of carbon-POSS membranes [51]. Therefore, we believe that the implementation of appropriate surface modification strategies can further improve the separation efficiency of C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes and expand their applications, which will be the underlying research direction. 4. Conclusions In this study, highly microporous C–SiO2–ZrO2 membranes were engineered by selectively utilizing phenyl-containing BFA as the OCL. The phenyl groups in the BFA act as rigid molecular spacers during the sol-gel process, facilitating the formation of a loose SiO2–ZrO2 network via steric hindrance and π-π interaction. High-temperature calcination induces the pyrolysis of organic components, thereby releasing the network pore while simultaneously enhancing membrane hydrophilic­ity.

content: e calcination induces the pyrolysis of organic components, thereby releasing the network pore while simultaneously enhancing membrane hydrophilic­ity. The AZB-700 membrane, derived through carbonization at 700 ◦C, combines a uniform microporous structure with hydrophilicity, exhib­iting exceptional MeOH/solvent PV separation performance due to the synergistic contributions of adsorption-diffusion and molecular sieving mechanisms. Specifically, the AZB-700 membrane demonstrated a MeOH permeance of 2.5 * 10-7 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and selectivity of 120 for MeOH/DMC (10/90 wt %), as well as a MeOH permeance of 3.7 *10-7 mol m-2 s-1 Pa-1 and selectivity of 1200 for MeOH/toluene (10/ 90 wt %). This study is expected to provide valuable insights into the tailored design of C–SiO2–ZrO2 membrane structures for extended sep­aration applications.
